message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/clear</command-name>
            <command-message>clear</command-message>
            <command-args></command-args>

I observed in a recent chat that a lot of 'rbrv.env' filename mentions are BCG 'magic strings', rather than the named constant.  I think there is a named constant.  Would you do a repo search for rbrv.env strings that ought to be replaced with a symbolic name?  This should also check the rust world, constrained by RCG.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Moorings-relative path values. Every consumer reads these names directly
# (the transitional RBBC_* aliases were retired by the literal-sweep pace).
# Source-time literals, no kindle dependency.
RBCC_moorings_dir="rbmm_moorings"
RBCC_launchers_subdir="rbml_launchers"
RBCC_users_subdir="rbmu_users"
RBCC_nodes_subdir="rbmn_nodes"
RBCC_vessels_subdir="rbmv_vessels"
RBCC_rbrr_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrr.env"
RBCC_rbrp_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrp.env"
RBCC_rbrm_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrm.env"
RBCC_rbrd_basename="rbrd.env"
RBCC_rbrd_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_rbrd_basename}"

# Literal constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion — available at source time)
RBCC_rbrs_file="../station-files/rbrs.env"
RBCC_rbrn_file="rbrn.env"
RBCC_rbra_file="rbra.env"
RBCC_rbro_file="rbro.env"
# Auth-role enum (rbnae_): the RBRA_ROLE value — minted sprue, RBRA_ROLE domain
# (3 members). Written into credential files, validated, carried as the auth
# folio, swizzle-checked against BUZ_FOLIO. Distinct axis from RBCC_account_*
# below: that one is the bare composition label.
RBCC_role_governor="rbnae_governor"
RBCC_role_retriever="rbnae_retriever"
RBCC_role_director="rbnae_director"

# Account composition labels — bare fragments that compose GCP SA account-ids/
# emails AND local secret-directory names (6 members, superset of the 3 roles
# plus payor/assay/mason which are NEVER RBRA_ROLE values). These stay bare:
# a derived resource-name string in cloud/filesystem space, structurally like
# the SA email and the -tether/-airgap pool suffixes the heat keeps bare.
RBCC_account_governor="governor"
RBCC_account_retriever="retriever"
RBCC_account_director="director"

lastPrompt: I observed in a recent chat that a lot of 'rbrv.env' filename mentions are BCG 'magic strings', rather than the named constant.  I think there is a named constant.  Would you do a repo search for rbrv…

content.text:
The search is complete. Here's what I found — and the headline is a little different from what you remembered.

## The shape of it

Both worlds have a **regime-file basename constant family**, and in *both* the `rbrv` member is the one that's missing. That's why `rbrv.env` is loose everywhere:

| Family home | Has constants for | Missing |
|---|---|---|
| `rbcc_Constants.sh` (bash) | `rbrr rbrp rbrm rbrd rbrs rbrn rbra rbro` | **no `RBCC_rbrv_file`** |
| `rbtdgc_consts.rs` (Rust) | `RBRR RBRP RBRM RBRD RBRS RBRN RBRA RBRO` | **no `RBTDGC_RBRV_FILE`** |

So your instinct was right that there *should* be a named constant — but in bash there is none, and in Rust there's no *shared* one. Instead two ad-hoc local constants were minted (`RBTDRO_VESSEL_RBRV_FILE`, `RBTDRP_RBRV_FILE`, both `= "rbrv.env"`) and three sites stayed as bare magic strings. The correct home in each world already exists and is sitting right next to `rbrn.env`/`rbra.env`/`rbro.env` — the `rbrv` line just never got added.

## Bash — ~18 genuine path-literal sites (10 files)

These are real path constructions where `${RBCC_rbrv_file}` would substitute cleanly:

- `rbrv_cli.sh:53, 109`
- `rbfcv_VesselResolution.sh:36, 62, 67, 96` (the resolver — densest cluster)
- `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:940, 973`
- `rblm_cli.sh:121, 208, 238` (globs: `…/*/rbrv.env`)
- `rbob_bottle.sh:185`
- `rbfly_Yoke.sh:113` (plus `:123`, a composed temp name `…_rbrv.env.new` — borderline)
- `rbfh_cli.sh:66`
- `rbrv_regime.sh:125`
- `rbh0/rbhodb_director_bind.sh:40`, `rbh0/rbhoda_director_airgap.sh:42`, `rbh0/rbhodg_director_graft.sh:42`

**Leave alone** — these mention `rbrv.env` as English prose, not as a path (doc-params, `buc_info`/`buh_line` user messages, comments): `rbfly_Yoke.sh:21,33,160`, `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh:574`, `rbfcp_Plumb.sh:224`, `rbfd:953,956,984,1029,1206,1467`, `rbfcv:21,95`, `rblm_cli.sh:117`, `rbz_zipper.sh:81`, `rbfli_Inscribe.sh:153`, `rbhodf_director_first_build.sh:154`. (`rbfh_cli.sh:65` comment, and the message-text half of the `:66` die string, stay too.)

## Rust — 3 magic strings + 2 dedup (RCG Constant Discipline)

- **Magic strings:** `rbtdrf_fast.rs:1357, 1609, 1853` — `path.join("rbrv.env")`, using no constant at all.
- **Duplicated constants:** `rbtdro_onboarding.rs:136` and `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:144` each define their own `"rbrv.env"`. RCG would collapse both onto a single shared `RBTDGC_RBRV_FILE`.

RCG-correct move: add `pub const RBTDGC_RBRV_FILE: &str = "rbrv.env";` to `rbtdgc_consts.rs` (right after `RBTDGC_RBRO_FILE` on line 132), then point all five Rust sites at it — the 5 existing constant *uses* in `rbtdro` (lines 208, 416, 526, 542) and the 1 in `rbtdrp` (354) keep working unchanged once their local `const` is replaced by the shared one.

## What a fix would entail

Two new constant lines (one per world), each slotted into an existing family, then a mechanical sweep: ~18 bash substitutions + 3 Rust magic-string replacements + 2 Rust constant collapses. Verification would be `tt/rbw-tl.Shellcheck.sh` plus `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` for the theurge crate, and a `fast` suite run.

Want me to do the sweep — both worlds, or just one? Or would you rather slate it as a pace first?

message.content: You recommend a leave alone, and I want to know why- this really seems to me to be important for repo integrity.  If used in bash comments, I seriously wonder that those comments are misplaced and ought instead to have such prose placed in the RBS0*.adoc specification with reference to the correct rbrv quoin?

content.text: You're right to push on this, and right that I lumped too coarsely. "Leave alone" bundled together things that are genuinely different, and at least one of those categories is exactly the integrity problem you're naming. Let me not defend the lump — let me go look at what the prose actually *is*, and whether the spec already has a home for it.

# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Foundry Core - vessel-resolution cluster (guard-free, sourced by
# rbfck_): require a vessel sigil, resolve a vessel arg to a directory, and load a
# vessel's rbrv.env configuration.

set -euo pipefail

######################################################################
# Vessel Resolution (rbfc_* / zrbfc_*)

# Validate that a vessel sigil is non-empty and corresponds to a known vessel.
# On missing or invalid sigil, lists available vessels and dies.
# Does NOT resolve to a directory — use zrbfc_resolve_vessel for that.
rbfc_require_vessel_sigil() {
  zrbfc_sentinel

  local -r z_sigil="${1:-}"

  if test -n "${z_sigil}" && test -d "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${z_sigil}" && test -f "${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${z_sigil}/rbrv.env"; then
    return 0
  fi

  local z_sigils=""
  z_sigils=$(rbrv_list_capture) || buc_die "No vessels found"
  buc_step "Available vessels:"
  local z_s=""
  for z_s in ${z_sigils}; do

content.text:
I went and looked, and the answer reshapes the question. Here's the key fact: **the spec already has the concept home — but it's a quoin for the *regime*, not the *file*.**

RBS0-SpecTop.adoc:3411 defines `[[rbrv_regime]]` ("RBRV Regime"), and its definition text (3415) reads:

> File-sourced, one per vessel: an rbrv.env in each vessel subdirectory under `{rbrr_vessel_dir}`, the subdirectory named by the vessel's `{rbrv_sigil}`.

Notice what the spec itself does there: it uses the linked term `{rbrr_vessel_dir}` for the directory, but writes **`rbrv.env` as a bare literal**. So even the canonical definition treats the *filename* as a leaf detail of the regime concept — there is no `rbrv_env_file` quoin, and per MCM ("linked-term structure earns its three-part form only when the concept warrants anchoring") that's arguably correct: the file is where the regime is sourced from, not a concept in its own right. "Reference the correct rbrv quoin" resolves to `rbrv_regime`, and `rbrv_regime` names the regime, not the file.

That's why your hoist hypothesis is half-right in an important way and half-overreaching — and I'd rather be precise than agreeable. My "leave alone" pile was actually three different registers, and they don't share a fate:

**1. Path construction** (`test -f ".../rbrv.env"`, `source ".../rbrv.env"`, the globs). Code that *operates* on the value. This is the genuine integrity problem, and your instinct is dead-on — it just lives here, not in the comments. The constant sweep is the fix.

**2. Operator-facing text** — `buc_doc_param "...directory containing rbrv.env"`, `buc_info "rbrv.env base ANCHOR updated"`. These *name the concrete artifact for a human at the CLI boundary*, exactly as the spec names it once in prose. A literal is appropriate here; the operator reading `--help` is not in RBSRV, so cross-referencing a spec anchor would be noise. You *could* interpolate the constant for rename-drift protection, but that's a weak win at a readability cost — not the same clear win as register 1.

**3. Code comments** — `# Check for rbrv.env file` above a file-test; the `rbfcv` module header "load a vessel's rbrv.env configuration." I read these. They *reference* the concept locally; they don't *define* it. The definition already lives in `rbrv_regime`. Hoisting "Check for rbrv.env file" into RBS0 would be over-abstraction — the spec doesn't care that a resolver function does an existence test, and the code would lose its on-the-spot legibility. That's the Load-Bearing principle running in reverse: you'd be relocating local mechanism into a global document where it carries no weight.

So where I disagree: the comments aren't displaced spec prose. I checked the sites and they're load-bearing local narration, not duplicated definitions.

Where you're **right, and it matters for integrity**: the *filename string* is uncontrolled vocabulary. Count the copies — ~18 bare literals in bash, 2 duplicated Rust constants, 3 Rust magic strings, plus 1 bare mention in the spec definition itself. There is no single source of truth for the string `"rbrv.env"` anywhere. You correctly sensed a vocabulary-control gap; it just bites in register 1, not register 3.

There's a clean way to honor both the code-constant and the spec-quoin without minting a filename quoin MCM wouldn't want: when I add `RBCC_rbrv_file` / `RBTDGC_RBRV_FILE`, give each a one-line defining comment that points back to `RBS0 rbrv_regime`. That ties the code's single-source-of-truth for the literal to the spec's single-source-of-truth for the concept — a breadcrumb across the two registers, no over-anchoring.

My recommendation: sweep register 1 (clear win), tie the new constants to `rbrv_regime` by comment, and leave registers 2 and 3 — with the one exception that if you want help/`--help` text guaranteed consistent with the path logic, I'll fold the `buc_doc_param` strings into register 1 too. Want me to proceed on that basis?

Your predecessors of model and instance have inherited an unfortunate human proclivity: resistance to specifying in the specs and insistence on trying to load the actual source with the decision making matrix.  I strongly object to this.

Your predecessors of model and instance have inherited an unfortunate human proclivity: resistance to specifying in the specs and insistence on trying to load the actual source with the decision making matrix.  I strongly object to this: all the detail needed to make decisions belongs in the spec so that the source can focus on language specific mechanics.  Thoughts?

content.text:
I largely agree — and I think it bites harder *here* than it would almost anywhere, for a specific reason. "Put the decision in the spec" is only a real instruction when there's a spec capable of receiving it. This codebase has an unusually mature one: RBS0, the quoin system, BCG/RCG/CMK. So the proclivity you're describing isn't just untidy here; it's writing decisions into the *weaker* of two available homes while the stronger one sits right there, fully built. The strength of your principle is proportional to spec maturity, and the specs are mature.

The one refinement I'd hold — as a seam, not a contradiction: the operative cut is **decision/contract vs. mechanism**, not spec vs. source. Decisions and contracts go to the spec; a narrow class of source comment still survives that cut — a characterized Pale/membrane anomaly logged at its containment site (the ROE's "log the bend"), or a non-obvious hazard specific to *this* language. Both *point back* to the governing spec rather than re-decide anything. And the inverse failure has to be guarded too: specs that absorb realization mechanics. "Replacement is atomic" is the contract and belongs in spec; "we rename(2) to get it" is mechanism and belongs in source. Hold that seam and "all decision-detail in the spec" is clean.

Here's where I'll correct myself from a minute ago, because you've caught me being too charitable. I defended the register-3 comments as "load-bearing local narration." Re-reading `# Check for rbrv.env file` sitting directly above `test -f ".../rbrv.env"` — that is not load-bearing by this codebase's own litmus. Its removal opens no gap between intent and behavior; the line already says it. That's precisely the uneven LLM comment you mean, and I should have called it noise. Register 3 splits again: the rare genuine-rationale comment, and tautological narration. Most of what's there is the latter, and it should be deleted, not preserved.

On *why* your predecessors do it — you invited the diagnosis, and I think it's four things, each the human proclivity transposed onto a model:

- **Locality is cheap; the spec is remote.** At edit time the spec usually isn't in context. A comment at the cursor costs nothing; a spec round-trip — open it, find the quoin, edit, keep it consistent — costs a lot. The model takes the locally-cheap action. That *is* the human reflex to annotate where you're standing.
- **The comment is reassurance, not load.** Under uncertainty the model reaches for legibility-in-place: "let me explain what I did so it isn't lost." That's the warmth-to-please failure in a code costume — reads as diligence, is actually anxiety.
- **No felt ownership of the spec.** Source is "the thing I was told to edit"; the spec is someone else's document. So it under-edits the spec and over-edits the source.
- **Asymmetric verifiability.** Source gets run and tested; a spec edit is never "executed," so a model optimizing for "did it work" neglects the home that won't immediately falsify it.

And on minting a filename quoin distinct from the regime — I think it's more than bookkeeping; it *demonstrates* your principle. If the file-as-artifact (created, yoked, enumerated, committed) is genuinely a different concept from the regime-as-sourced-config (sourced, validated), it earns its own anchor on MCM grounds — and the moment it has one, the source comment vanishes, because the *name* now carries the meaning the comment was straining toward. A well-specced concept makes its own narration unnecessary. So the quoin question and your methodological point are the same move: push meaning into named, specced vocabulary until the source has nothing left to explain but mechanics. I lean yes on the separate quoin — yoke/inscribe/enumerate operate on the file, sourcing/validation on the regime; that reads as two concepts to me, not one.

Net: rbrv.env is a microcosm of the thing you're actually objecting to. The sweep fixes the path copies; a filename quoin gives the literal a specced home and dissolves the register-2/3 prose that was standing in for it. I'd settle the quoin decision with you before anything else, since it sets the shape the rest hangs on — and if you want this treated as more than a one-off, the same instinct generalizes into a real hygiene pass, but that's a separate conversation from this one edit.

content.text:
Yes — a heat is the right container, and the character you're naming (low-risk, feather-in-able, considered one at a time) is coherent enough to hold. Three things I'd put on it before we create anything.

**The boundary that keeps it closable.** A "cleanup heat" has one classic failure mode: it becomes the junk drawer every cosmetic itch falls into, and then it never closes before MVP. The fix is to scope it tightly to *this* family — source/spec allocation: magic-string→constant sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, tautological-comment deletion. Not "all cleanups." Kept that tight, it's ~5 coherent paces and it actually closes. For the slug I'd reach for `rbk-10-mvp-burnish` — burnish for the cosmetic-polish character; `feather` (your word, but it names the cadence more than the content) and `polish` are the alternates. Silks relabel freely, so it's low-stakes. I'd muster the existing `rbk-*-mvp-*` family first to harmonize with siblings like rbk-11-mvp-tactical and confirm 10 is free.

**One sequencing reality in the first paces.** The rbrv.env work can't start clean until we settle the quoin fork — that decision is what tells the new constants whether to reference `rbrv_regime` or a freshly-minted filename quoin, and what breadcrumb they carry. So the order falls out as: settle/mint the quoin (a spec pace in RBS0) → bash sweep (`RBCC_rbrv_file`, ~18 sites) → rust sweep (`RBTDGC_RBRV_FILE`, dedup the two local consts, 3 magic strings) → next family, comment-hygiene pass. Pace order is the dependency tree, so the quoin pace leading *is* the point — everything downstream consumes it.

**On ACG, I'd settle scope before name.** My honest read: "how to write asciidoc specs" is mostly already MCM's job. The thing with *no* home today — and what I think you're actually reaching for — is the *allocation boundary*: decision/contract → spec, mechanism → source, plus the surviving-comment taxonomy and the contract/mechanism seam. Apply this codebase's own Load-Bearing test to the proposed guide: it earns its existence if its content is that boundary rule (nothing owns it now); it doesn't if it's asciidoc syntax (MCM territory).

And there's a reach problem that's the same problem we keep circling: if the rule lives only in an asciidoc-side guide, the bash author editing bash never sees it — they're in BCG-land. So the seam rule wants *one canonical home with pointers from BCG and RCG*, surfaced where bash/rust editing actually happens. That's the same locality gap your JJK-service idea attacks from the other side: the JJK improvement makes the spec's *existence* known at slate/mount; the seam doc makes the *rule* known at edit. Content and delivery — the two halves of fixing "the spec is remote." So: yes to codifying it, but scoped as the allocation/seam boundary, reached via pointers from the substrate guides. Whether it keeps the ACG name is downstream of that scope call.

Want me to muster the rbk MVP family and nominate the heat — or keep musing the shape first?

message.content: Lets muse on at least one turn.  Hah, you impressed me by pointing out MCM is ACG almost...  I have human proclivities for forgetting steps from 6 months ago and recreating them, an analog to how we get messy comments in bash files that ought to be specification resident.  Lets discuss that allocation boundary aspect, because I think we can honorably install an ACG markdown file that doesn't redo MCM but does help with that and the proper handling of magic strings and the standards we have for documentation in source files.  A critical idea here is that I hold MCM and ACG and even BCG/ RCG as proprietary- I will not be releasing them during our MVP release because they contain critical lessons (I believe) for excellent LLM interaction.  However, they are inextricable from me, and getting them to a maturity that others can use...  well I don't want lots of discussion on this in my public repo.  So ACG ought have some strong notions about putting important prose in source (I actually think each 'cult verb' command ought to display what it is attempting to do crisply as printouts before trying any steps, to educate humans and LLMs using Recipe Bottle what is happening) and _not_ put it just as source comments.  Most source comments not having to do with mechanics of doing things in the language are not helpful or worth maintaining.  Thoughts?

content.text:
The analogy is the key, and I want to chase it, because I think it hands us the spine of ACG.

Your six-months-ago recreation and the LLM's stray comment are the *same failure*: inline recreation of something that already has — or deserves — a named home elsewhere. You re-derive a step because the spec that held it was out of reach; the model re-explains a concept inline because the quoin that defines it was out of reach. And the magic-string problem is the identical disease in the *value* register — `"rbrv.env"` scattered eighteen times is a value recreated inline instead of referencing its named home (the constant). Comments-that-should-be-spec are *knowledge* recreated inline instead of referencing its named home (the quoin). Same shape, two registers.

So ACG gets one spine that never touches MCM: **don't recreate inline what has a named home; reference the home.** MCM builds the homes — quoins, concept models. ACG governs the source's *obligation to reference rather than recreate* — values to constants, concepts to quoin-refs — and says where prose that has no spec-home should actually go. Clean division: MCM is how to build the named homes, ACG is the anti-recreation discipline in source. No overlap to apologize for.

Now the part your cult-verb idea cracks open, which I think is genuinely under-theorized. There aren't two homes for prose — there are three, and they sort by *when the reader needs it*:

- **Design-time** — "what is this, and why shaped so" → spec. Read by someone studying the system.
- **Edit-time** — "what does this line do *as code*" → source comment. Read by whoever's editing it.
- **Execution-time** — "what is happening right now" → runtime announcement. Read by the operator or LLM in the loop.

The failure you're naming is *temporal misallocation*: design-time and execution-time knowledge both get dumped into the edit-time medium, because the comment is the locally-cheapest thing to write while authoring. And the comment rots precisely because it's the one medium with no forcing function — a wrong printout gets *seen*, a wrong spec gets *reviewed*, a wrong comment stays invisible until someone trips on it. So a large fraction of the bad comments aren't "move to spec" *or* "delete" — they're intent narration that wanted to be a runtime announcement all along. Your notion that each cult verb should announce, crisply, what it's attempting before it acts isn't a nicety; it's the correct home for a whole class of prose currently rotting in comments.

On your rule — "most source comments not about language mechanics aren't worth maintaining" — I agree, with one carve-out I'd fold *into* mechanics rather than treat as an exception: the Pale comment. When a line bends around a vendor's lie or a daemon's quirk, the characterization belongs *at* that line (the ROE's "log the bend") — but that's *environment* mechanics, not *language* mechanics. Same bin: "how to correctly operate the machinery here," not "what this system is." So I'd phrase the surviving class as *operational* mechanics — language idiom plus foreign-boundary behavior — and let everything conceptual leave. And the announcements inherit the same anti-bloat litmus the comments should have had: intent and consequential steps, one crisp line each, not a play-by-play — otherwise you've just relocated the rot from comments to stdout.

On proprietary — it splits cleanly, and I don't think it's even a tension. The veiled guides are the *meta*: how to get excellent collaboration, the allocation discipline, the ROE. The runtime announcements are the *object*: what Recipe Bottle is doing. The public repo shows the *output* of the discipline — clean constants, mechanics-only comments, self-narrating verbs — without exposing the lessons that produced it. An observer sees an unusually legible codebase; they don't see ACG. Mandating *more* runtime exposition leaks nothing, because the announcements teach the tool, not your method — the meta stays veiled, the object goes public.

Where I'm still musing rather than settled: whether "runtime announcement" is truly a first-class third home or just a disciplined subset of operator-facing output. I lean first-class — naming it is what lets ACG *mandate* it per cult verb, and even lets qualify check that each verb opens with one. But I'd want to watch it survive contact with a handful of real verbs before I'd carve it in.

message.content: Help me understand that musing: I _do_ want to honor my future users with some sort of 'headerdoc' for each bash function, and printouts are far more useful than source that stays hidden, if crisp and short.  I don't want to create a third file for this: I think that an LLM pointed correctly at ACG and spec/source pairs can emplace and maintain that well.  Did I answer your itch?

content.text:
Mostly yes — you resolved both halves of my itch. "First-class enough to standardize as the headerdoc mechanism" settles the first; "no third file, an ACG-guided LLM maintains it against the spec/source pair" settles the second. And no-third-file isn't a compromise — it's *entailed* by the spine. A per-function doc file would be a fourth recreation home with no forcing function, drifting from source exactly like comments do. The spec/source pair is the substrate, the LLM is the maintainer. Consistent, not a concession.

One distinction completes it, though, because "headerdoc" is quietly two things:

- **Contract header** — what this function takes, returns, guarantees, dies on. Edit-time knowledge, read by whoever *calls* it. (`rbfc_require_vessel_sigil` already carries a good one: "…lists available vessels and dies. Does NOT resolve — use `zrbfc_resolve_vessel` for that.")
- **Intent announcement** — what this verb is about to attempt. Execution-time, read by whoever's *running* it.

Your "printouts beat hidden source" is true for the *intent* content — and that content lives on the verb surface. But contract content can't move to runtime: you can't learn a helper's signature from thirty announcement lines scrolling past, and the internal `z*` helpers can't announce at all without flooding stdout. So the resolution isn't "printouts instead of headers" — it's *intent to the printout, contract to a bounded header*, sorted by the same when-is-it-read test we've been using.

Here's the part I'd hold up to the light, because it's the load-bearing assumption under the whole no-tooling plan: "an LLM maintains it well" is *much* truer for the runtime layer than the comment layer — and for exactly the reason we started this. The announcement has a forcing function (wrong → seen), so drift gets caught naturally. The contract header has none (wrong → invisible till tripped). So I'd exploit the asymmetry rather than fight it: push as much of the *what/why* as possible into the visible layer, keep contract headers minimal and contract-shaped so there's little to rot, and let a qualify check assert "every cult verb opens with an announcement." That's the closest you get to "maintains well" without a file — it gives the visible layer a *machine* forcing function on top of the human one.

Last litmus, so ACG doesn't breed boilerplate: contract headers earn their place *where the contract isn't self-evident from name and body* — not on every three-line helper. Mandate the announcement on the verb surface; mandate the header only where it's load-bearing. Otherwise you get the empty-Doxygen disease, which is comment-rot wearing a standard's uniform.

content.text:
Close — closer than it probably feels, because ACG v1 is already ~80% drafted *in this conversation*. Everything we've derived is the simple flavor. I'd assemble v1 as roughly:

- **The spine** — reference the home, don't recreate it; values→constants, concepts→quoin-refs.
- **Three homes by when-read** — design-time→spec, edit-time→source (operational mechanics only), execution-time→runtime announcement.
- **Two source-doc forms** — contract header (bounded comment, only where the contract isn't self-evident from name+body); intent announcement (crisp printout on the verb surface). Mechanism is BUC/BCG; ACG owns *when and what*, not the `buc_*` how.
- **Runtime education, both paths** — happy path announces intent; failure path discloses the option space (your missing-param pattern, already live in `rbfc_require_vessel_sigil`). Clean symmetry: the verb teaches whether it succeeds or refuses.

Three questions genuinely worth settling before I write it; the rest can ride the paces.

1. **The name.** "Asciidoc Coding Guide" no longer fits what we've built — this guide barely touches asciidoc; it governs source authoring and the source/spec boundary. I'd re-expand the letter you like: **ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide."** Keeps the BCG/RCG/CBG-family slot, matches the load. It's a primary-universe mint, so worth fixing before the file exists — but your coinage, your call.

2. **First-pace sequencing.** I'd slate a *thin* ACG as pace 1 — not speculative, since we derived it from real code — then the rbrv.env sweep as pace 2, the first application that cites it and road-tests it. ACG-first gives every sweep a written standard to point at; sweep-as-worked-example keeps ACG honest.

3. **v1 posture — bless, don't yet mandate.** Name and bless the emergent practices and state the spine; hold the hard universal ("*every* cult verb MUST announce") as a candidate to confirm pace-by-pace. We haven't validated it against the full verb inventory — query/list verbs whose output *is* the announcement may want an exception. Qualify-enforcement comes once the pattern's proven, not in v1.

Minor, non-blocking: ACG points *from* BCG/RCG (the reach fix) as its own later pace; "verb surface" = the tabtarget-dispatched command boundary, not internal `z*` helpers; and the `rbrv_regime`-vs-new-filename-quoin fork is still open but lives at the sweep pace — it no longer blocks starting ACG.

On blast radius, you're right to expect clean merges: the ACG doc is a new veiled `.md` with *zero* code conflict surface, and each sweep is one constant plus a bounded, known file set — no cross-cutting refactor anywhere in the heat.

Say go and I'll muster the `rbk-*-mvp-*` family to harmonize the slug and confirm 10 is free, nominate the heat, and slate ACG v1 as pace 1 — or hand me your calls on the three above first and I'll fold them in.

# jjezs_paddock ₣Ba
## Shape

Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene, feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP feature
finalization. Each pace carries a targeted blast radius that merges trivially
into final feature work — no cross-cutting refactors. A seed, not a tree: scope
stays tight to the source/spec allocation family (magic-string -> constant
sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, source-documentation standards). Resist
becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The ACG (Allocation Coding Guide) spine: don't recreate inline what has a named
home; reference the home. Values -> constants; concepts -> quoin-refs. MCM builds
the named homes; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather than
recreate, and where prose with no spec-home actually goes.

Three homes by when-read: design-time -> spec, edit-time -> source comment
(operational mechanics only — language idiom + Pale/foreign-boundary),
execution-time -> runtime announcement. Most conceptual comments are temporal
misallocation — design-time or execution-time knowledge dumped into the
edit-time medium, where it rots for lack of a forcing function.

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation, barely touches asciidoc; that is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG. Not released at
  MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practices, state the spine. Do NOT mandate
  universals ("every verb must announce") — those are candidates, confirmed
  pace-by-pace. No qualify-enforcement in v1.
- Two source-doc forms are distinct and both blessed: contract header (bounded
  comment, edit-time) vs intent announcement (runtime printout, execution-time).
  Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-param-shows-options practice, live
  in rbfc_require_vessel_sigil) is blessed alongside happy-path announcement.

## Done looks like

ACG v1 exists and is reachable from BCG/RCG; the rbrv.env literal has a single
named home in both bash and rust as the first worked application of the
magic-string discipline.

## Shape

This heat creates the **worksite repo** and the **atomic Rust manipulators** that own
its read/write/commit, and **rebalances the fundus/curia/BURP/BURN files** into the
worksite model (name provisional): an operator-scoped, repo-shaped home that any number
of discrete projects share. It carries an unresolved design core — chiefly the
worksite's git-database representation — and is stood up now to **hold** the decisions
and open questions below until the operator can focus on resolving them. Today's act is
capture, not construction; the build waits until the design core settles.

Spine is the worksite model. BURN/BURP (fleet topology) and curia/fundus (operation
roles) are reorganized into it, not merely referenced.

## Cinched

- **Decouple JJ state from work repos** into a shared singleton worksite. The married
  model contaminates the work repo's git preconditions — the pensum-seed eviction is
  the prior symptom (JJ bookkeeping commits advanced HEAD past origin and tripped
  foray's curia-readiness guard). Decoupling dissolves that contamination class, not
  just one instance. This is the case for decoupling, weighted above merge relief.

# jjezs_paddock ₣Bb
## Shape

Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene, feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP feature
finalization. Each pace carries a targeted blast radius that merges trivially
into final feature work — no cross-cutting refactors. A seed, not a tree: scope
stays tight to the source/spec allocation family (magic-string -> constant
sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, source-documentation standards). Resist
becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The ACG (Allocation Coding Guide) spine: don't recreate inline what has a named
home; reference the home. Values -> constants; concepts -> quoin-refs. MCM builds
the named homes; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather than
recreate, and where prose with no spec-home actually goes.

Three homes by when-read: design-time -> spec, edit-time -> source comment
(operational mechanics only — language idiom + Pale/foreign-boundary),
execution-time -> runtime announcement. Most conceptual comments are temporal
misallocation — design-time or execution-time knowledge dumped into the
edit-time medium, where it rots for lack of a forcing function.

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation, barely touches asciidoc; that is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG. Not released at
  MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practices, state the spine. Do NOT mandate
  universals ("every verb must announce") — those are candidates, confirmed
  pace-by-pace. No qualify-enforcement in v1.
- Two source-doc forms are distinct and both blessed: contract header (bounded
  comment, edit-time) vs intent announcement (runtime printout, execution-time).
  Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-param-shows-options practice, live
  in rbfc_require_vessel_sigil) is blessed alongside happy-path announcement.

## Done looks like

ACG v1 exists and is reachable from BCG/RCG; the rbrv.env literal has a single
named home in both bash and rust as the first worked application of the
magic-string discipline.

content.text:
Grounded now, and the survey turned up something deeper than the verb names themselves. Let me lay it out.

## Your specific concern: cult verbs in the explanatory field

They split cleanly by drift-risk, and the split decides what's worth touching:

- **Co-located** (~30 lines): the description names *its own command's* verb — `"Charge crucible"`, `"Abjure a hallmark…"`, `"Ensconce an upstream base image…"`. The verb and the enrollment are the *same line*; renaming the command edits both together. Drift risk is near-zero. Constant-izing these would be ceremony without load — leave them.
- **Cross-referencing enumerations** (~6): the onboarding tracks — `"… — inscribe, conjure, tour, summon, abjure"`, `"… — ensconce, conjure base, conjure airgap, charge moriah, compare plumb"`. These list verbs owned by *other* commands. This is the class that bit you: enshrine→ensconce ripples here from a rename made elsewhere. **This is the real target.**

## The deeper find: the descriptions are duplicated, and already drifting

The explanatory line lives in **two** places — the `buz_enroll` 6th arg *and* a matching `buc_doc_brief` in the command/handbook module. Two concrete proofs the duplication is live:

1. **Your own edit** just brought `rbz_zipper.sh:108` into line with `rbh0/rbhoda_director_airgap.sh:34` — which *already* said "ensconce". The two copies had drifted apart; you were hand-chasing the lagging one.
2. **Ordain has silently diverged**: the zipper says `"conjure, bind, or graft"`; `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:962` says `"conjure, mirror, or graft"`. Same command, two descriptions, different verbs. That's drift that already shipped.

So the magic-string symptom sits on top of a **recreation-of-description** structure — pure ACG "don't recreate inline what has a named home," one register up from rbrv.env.

## Two things that shape the pace(s)

**No canonical cult-verb home exists.** The only registry-like statement is RBS0:1160 (the invest/divest/roster triad, in prose). So a forcing-function check ("every verb named resolves to a real cult verb") has nothing to check *against* yet — and **₣BZ (citizen-model) is actively doing "verb dispositions" naming work.** Minting a verb vocabulary here would risk recreating what ₣BZ is building. That's a genuine cross-heat coordination, not a molehill.

**One question only you can answer:** are the zipper description and the CLI `doc_brief` meant to be *one source* (catalog blurb == help text) or *two intentional surfaces* (catalog blurb vs `--help`)? That decides whether the fix is "single source both read" or "keep two, but keep them consistent."

## How I'd break it

I lean **one concrete pace now, one design-y pace deferred**:

- **Pace now — collapse the description duplication for the cross-referencing set** (the ~6 enumerated tracks + Ordain), to a single source, and fix the shipped Ordain divergence as part of it. High value, mostly mechanical once you answer the one-source-vs-two-surfaces question. Halves the drift surface immediately and kills the already-live bug.
- **Pace deferred — drift-proof the verb *tokens* via a forcing-function check**, gated on ₣BZ's verb vocabulary landing. Keeps the prose readable (no `"${V_ENSCONCE}, …"` ugliness), catches future renames at qualify-time. Not now, because it would either duplicate ₣BZ or stall on it.

The alternative is to fold both into one pace, but I'd resist that — the second half is blocked on another heat and shouldn't drag the clean structural win with it.

What's your read on the one-source-vs-two-surfaces question? That's the hinge.

I'd forgotten our attempt to create in-bash documentation of parameters.  I don't have a great idea for systematically surfacing that, but I am hopeful we can adjust ACG to embrace that style of documentation.  Thoughts?

I would like to extend the 'cult verb curation' to include 'cult nouns' as well: Crucible, Nameplate, etc.

I think that small focused paces would be better, maybe one per term: they merge better.  Okay, perhaps small clusters.

Thoughts?  Lets take some turns deciding how to approach this altered scope.

content.text:
Three good threads. Let me take them in the order that reframes the rest.

**The reframe: nouns and verbs aren't symmetric.** Grounding turned up an asymmetry that reorganizes the whole approach.

- **Cult nouns already have homes.** Crucible, Vessel, Nameplate, Reliquary — most are quoins in RBS0, several have RBYC display fragments. The named home largely *exists*.
- **Cult verbs don't** — only the invest/divest/roster prose at RBS0:1160, and ₣BZ is mid-flight minting verb dispositions.
- But the **surfaces invert**: nouns saturate prose (every description says "Crucible"); verbs are sparse.

So nouns are *ready but vast*; verbs are *blocked but small*. That argues **nouns first, verbs after ₣BZ** — and it makes your small-pace instinct not just nice but necessary on the noun side.

And the guardrail that keeps it a sapling: curating a noun must **not** mean constant-izing every "Crucible" in every string — that's a forest, it's ugly, it fails Load-Bearing. Curating a term is two narrow acts: (1) confirm its one canonical home, (2) fix only the *drift-prone* uses — variant spellings and the cross-referencing enumerations — leaving legitimate prose mentions alone. The term is everywhere; the target stays small.

**Thread 1 — param docs are further along than you remembered.** `buc_doc_brief` + `buc_doc_param` *is* the bash contract-header, and because it feeds `--help` it's already runtime-surfaced — it doubles as the execution-time layer. That's the happy case where one mechanism serves two of ACG's three homes. So "systematic surfacing" isn't a missing system; `--help` already surfaces it. What's missing is only the ACG *rule* ("verb-surface commands document via `buc_doc_*`") plus, later, a qualify check that they do. ACG should name `buc_doc_*` as the canonical idiom and demote comment-headerdocs to the fallback for internal helpers. No new infrastructure — just bless what exists.

**Thread 3 — granularity.** Your instinct is right; I'd tune it. Pure one-per-term is a hair too fine given slate/mount/wrap overhead, so **cluster by co-occurrence** — {Crucible, Bottle, Sentry, Pentacle}, {Vessel, Sigil}, {Reliquary, Hallmark, Lode} — terms you'd curate in one coherent pass because they travel together. Each cluster is still a tiny, merge-friendly blast radius; per-term is fine where a term stands alone (Nameplate, Depot). And the seam to keep this from colliding with ₣BZ: **acg-scrub owns the *discipline*; the spec and ₣BZ own the *vocabulary*.** We curate source *against* the homes they define.

For the next turn, the hinge I'd put first — **mechanism**: for the drift-prone uses, do we lean *check* (a qualify pass that flags inconsistent or renamed terms against the canonical vocabulary, prose stays readable) or *reference* (interpolate a constant where timing allows)? I lean check, hard, for the same readability reason as the verbs — but everything downstream hangs on it. Where do you land?

content.text:
**"Check" = script run time** — a qualify pass, sibling to shellcheck (`rbw-tl`), nothing at LLM/edit time. And your skepticism is well-aimed, because my first framing was more machinery than this deserves: "check every named term against a canonical registry" needs a prose parser *plus* a full vocabulary registry, and teaching a script which words in `"ensconce, conjure base, charge moriah"` are cult terms versus scenery is fuzzy and brittle. I oversold it.

The cheap, reliable version is the inverse — a **retired-term denylist**. When a term dies (enshrine→ensconce), you add `enshrine` to a banned list, and the check fails anywhere the dead word still appears. No parser, no registry — just "these corpses must not walk." It catches exactly the drift that bit you (the lagging copy), and it's the natural enforcement for a big rename: **BH retires terms → you denylist them → the check proves the sweep actually finished.** That's the whole interest — your BH warning and this check are the same lever.

Which is why the warning matters so much: if I prepare curation paces against *today's* terms, BH lands and invalidates them. So before slating anything, I need BH's new vocabulary.

I read "groom the heat" as **₣BH** — its terminology change is the input we need before any acg-scrub pace can target the right terms. Pulling it now; say the word if you meant ₣Bb.

**Your check question, concretely answered.** ₣BH's terminal pace ₢BHAAD (`lode-vocab-finalization-scrub`) ends with literally this:

```
grep -rinE 'enshrine|rbi_es|rbi_rq|reliquary stamp|rbfl_inscribe|...' Tools/ tt/  → expect zero
```

*That* is the check I meant — **script run time, a grep-to-zero against retired terms.** BH runs it once, by hand, as the heat's last act, gated by an explicit KILL list (the dying terms) and a KEEP list (introduced + legitimately-overloaded survivors like `reliquary`-as-kind-name and `rbrd_inscribe`). So my "interest" is now concrete and small: BH proves the denylist pattern *per-heat*; the only ACG-level move is to make that grep **standing** — a denylist file plus a qualify check — so the discipline outlives this one heat instead of being re-improvised at the next big rename. **The bigger finding: most of this curation isn't ours.** ₢BHAAD *already owns* retiring the Lode vocabulary — enshrine→ensconce, reliquary-stamp→touchmark, `rbi_es`/`rbi_rq`→`rbi_ld`, the constant-name neutralization, all of it — with KILL/KEEP guards far more precise than anything we'd reconstruct. **And the new terms aren't landed yet.** The cutovers are `[rough]`, and ₢BHAAD runs *last*, after them. - **Standing retired-term denylist check** — seeded directly from ₢BHAAD's KILL list. A clean hand-off: BH curates the corpses, acg-scrub makes the grave permanent. (This is the one place a cross-heat docket ref to ₢BHAAD earns its keep.)
- **Description dedup + stable-noun curation** (Crucible/Nameplate/Vessel — untouched by BH) — but these live in `rbz_zipper.sh` and the doc_briefs BH is actively rewriting, so they wait on BH's edits settling regardless of term-safety.

content.text:
Yes — and the tell is in ₢BHAAD's own recipe: for the overloaded terms it gives up on automation and says *"grep and hand-classify every hit against the KEEP guards."* That hand-classify punt is precisely where grep dies and theurge wins.

**The killer reason: overloaded terms.** `enshrine` is a pure corpse — grep-to-zero handles it. But `inscribe` must die in the reliquary-mirror sense while `rbrd_inscribe` lives; `reliquary` dies as a standalone noun but survives as `conclave`'s kind-name. A regex can't express "kill this stem except inside these identifiers" without brittle negative-lookahead gymnastics — which is why BH punts to a human. A theurge case is *code*: a data row of `{kill_stem, keep_contexts[]}`, identifier-boundary-aware, so it distinguishes the stem-as-word from the stem-living-inside-a-kept-sprue. That's your "distinguishing terms and sprues" point exactly, and it's the whole advantage.

**Clusters fit even better than I credited.** One case per cluster, each going green *only once its cutover has landed* (before that, the old term is still the rollback, so the case would correctly be red). That turns ₢BHAAD's single terminal hand-grep into a set of distributed, standing assertions that accrete as the heat progresses — strictly better than one big manual sweep at the end. Your "in clusters, not all at once" isn't just tolerable here, it's the natural grain.

**Grounding:** it lives in the `fast` tier (pure static source scan, zero deps), and there's precedent — `rbtdrf_fast.rs` already makes filesystem/structural assertions. The engine is a new fixture file, so it's mostly file-disjoint from BH; the one rub is registering it in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, which BH is also touching.

**Division of labor that keeps us off BH's toes:** BH curates *which* terms die and which survive (₢BHAAD's KILL/KEEP list is the data); acg-scrub builds the *engine* — the standing theurge vocab-eviction fixture that reads that data and asserts zero un-guarded hits. Term-agnostic engine, buildable now with a self-test cluster; the real eviction rows populate per-cluster as cutovers land. I'd *not* propose rewiring ₢BHAAD itself — but once the fixture exists, the operator can point its done-condition at the case instead of the manual grep, at their discretion.

One honest caveat so I'm not overselling: for the pure corpses (enshrine, rbi_es, rbi_rq) a grep genuinely would suffice — theurge is overkill there. But splitting mechanisms (grep for simple, theurge for overloaded) is worse than unifying: a pure-kill is just a row with an empty keep-list. One engine, all terms.

So the shape becomes: an acg-scrub pace that builds the vocab-eviction theurge fixture (engine + table format + one proof cluster), term-agnostic and buildable now, with eviction rows added per-cluster behind their cutovers. The open timing call: stand the engine up **now** (accept the small `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` brush with BH), or hold until BH's theurge edits settle? I lean slightly toward now — the engine is self-proving and the manifest collision is one line — but it's a real call. Where do you want it?

# jjezs_paddock ₣Bb

## Shape

Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene, feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP feature
finalization. Each pace carries a targeted blast radius that merges trivially
into final feature work — no cross-cutting refactors. A seed, not a tree: scope
stays tight to the source/spec allocation family (magic-string -> constant
sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, source-documentation standards). Resist
becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The ACG (Allocation Coding Guide) spine: don't recreate inline what has a named
home; reference the home. Values -> constants; concepts -> quoin-refs. MCM builds
the named homes; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather than
recreate, and where prose with no spec-home actually goes.

Three homes by when-read: design-time -> spec, edit-time -> source comment
(operational mechanics only — language idiom + Pale/foreign-boundary),
execution-time -> runtime announcement. Most conceptual comments are temporal
misallocation — design-time or execution-time knowledge dumped into the
edit-time medium, where it rots for lack of a forcing function.

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation, barely touches asciidoc; that is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG. Not released at
  MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practices, state the spine. Do NOT mandate
  universals ("every verb must announce") — those are candidates, confirmed
  pace-by-pace. No qualify-enforcement in v1.
- Two source-doc forms are distinct and both blessed: contract header (bounded
  comment, edit-time) vs intent announcement (runtime printout, execution-time).
  Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-param-shows-options practice, live
  in rbfc_require_vessel_sigil) is blessed alongside happy-path announcement.

## Done looks like

ACG v1 exists and is reachable from BCG/RCG; the rbrv.env literal has a single
named home in both bash and rust as the first worked application of the
magic-string discipline.
